query: arrow vs regular functions javascript interview question

competitors.label: Arrow function expressions
competitors.selectionReason: MDN is the canonical reference for lexical this, missing arguments/prototype, and constructor limitations of arrow functions.
competitors.winReason: MDN is the canonical semantic reference, but our page is now stronger for the compare-and-choose interview query because it ties the choice to callbacks, event listeners, methods, constructors, and class-field memory trade-offs in one compact answer.
theirStrengths: It is authoritative on the actual runtime semantics: lexical this, no own arguments, no constructor support, and method caveats.
theirGaps: It is reference-oriented rather than tightly optimized for an interview-style compare-and-choose answer.

realWorldUseCases: Use arrow functions for short callbacks, array transforms, and places where lexical this is the safe default.
followUpConfusion: In classes, prototype methods (regular method syntax) are shared across instances. Arrow methods defined as class fields create a function per instance.

realWorldUseCases: Our page now maps the choice across callbacks, event listeners, methods, constructors, and class-field trade-offs more directly as an interview decision.
actionableExamples: Both pages include code that demonstrates this binding, detached callbacks, and constructor limitations.
followUpConfusion: Our page now answers a likely follow-up that MDN does not foreground as much in one place: why class-field arrows trade easier binding for per-instance memory cost.

actionableExamples: Add one tiny example of an arrow callback inside a class method to complement the event-listener case.

competitors.label: What advantage is there for using the JavaScript arrow syntax for a method in a constructor?
competitors.selectionReason: This is GreatFrontEnd's closest interview-prep page on arrow versus regular function behavior, focusing on call-site rebinding and constructor-defined methods.
competitors.winReason: GreatFrontEnd is strong on the constructor-method use case, but our page better matches the broader query by comparing arrows and regular functions across callbacks, methods, constructors, arguments, prototype, and common misuse cases.
theirStrengths: It uses strong runnable examples for this rebinding, detached callbacks, and constructor-defined arrow methods.
theirGaps: It is centered on one narrow use case rather than the broader choose-the-right-function decision.

realWorldUseCases: Use regular functions when you need dynamic this, constructor behavior, event-listener receiver semantics, or function-level features like arguments and prototype.
followUpConfusion: Putting arrow functions directly as object methods can surprise you when you expect dynamic receiver-based this.

realWorldUseCases: Our page is broader on practical use cases because it covers callbacks, methods, constructors, and function-level features, not just constructor-defined methods.
actionableExamples: Both pages include strong code examples for this-binding behavior, so the example criterion stays close.
followUpConfusion: Our answer is more explicit about method-definition pitfalls, prototype absence, and when regular functions are the right tool.

actionableExamples: Add one code example that contrasts an object method defined with concise method syntax versus an arrow property.

competitors.label: Arrow functions, the basics
competitors.selectionReason: javascript.info is a strong tutorial reference for arrow syntax, concise returns, and the baseline function-expression comparison.
competitors.winReason: javascript.info teaches arrow syntax cleanly, but our page is better for the actual compare-and-choose interview intent because it emphasizes when not to use arrows, dynamic this behavior, and prototype-related trade-offs.
theirStrengths: It is clear and approachable for syntax basics and concise one-line examples.
theirGaps: It is much lighter on the practical question of when regular functions are still the better choice.

realWorldUseCases: Use arrow functions for short callbacks, array transforms, and places where lexical this is the safe default.
followUpConfusion: Arrow functions cannot be constructors and have no prototype.

realWorldUseCases: Our page is more directly tied to practical selection decisions instead of mostly syntax introduction.
actionableExamples: javascript.info gives clearer step-by-step syntax examples for basic arrow forms and implicit return.
followUpConfusion: Our answer is stronger on the likely follow-up confusion about dynamic versus lexical this and method-definition pitfalls.
